pattern: Every boundary between two neighboring characters that disagree is a fault line that must be closed by a prefix or suffix flip, and each fault line can be closed independently by whichever side of it is cheaper.
hints: Think about what happens at each boundary between two neighboring characters, rather than about the string as a whole.

A prefix flip up to index i and a suffix flip starting at index i+1 are the only two operations that change whether s[i] and s[i+1] look equal to each other -- and each one leaves every other boundary in the string untouched.

For every boundary where the two neighboring characters differ, you must pay to flip one side of it; pick whichever side (the prefix ending at i, or the suffix starting at i+1) is cheaper, and sum that over every differing boundary.

edge_inputs.description: single differing boundary in the middle
two separate blocks of differing boundaries, costs favor different sides
shortest possible string with one differing boundary
longer alternating string near the oracle-checkable size
already all equal, cost must be zero
mixed pattern where cheapest side isn't always the same one

stress.description: random short binary strings, kept small so the shortest-path oracle stays fast
second seed for extra coverage of small random binary strings
